mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that provides declarative APIs and tooling to simplify the provisioning, upgrading, and management of multiple Kubernetes clusters. It is particularly useful for platform operators who need to manage clusters across various infrastructure environments. Key trade-offs include the complexity of setup and the need for familiarity with Kubernetes concepts.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This repository contains the necessary charts for installation.

steps.title: Install Cluster API Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are required for Cluster API to function properly. This step installs them into your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

> This YAML file contains all necessary CRDs.

steps.title: Install Cluster API components
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API components into the specified namespace.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

```

> Ensure you have the correct namespace set up.
commands: helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

steps.title: Create a Cluster API management cluster
steps.description:
This step creates a management cluster that will be used to manage other clusters.

```bash

kubectl create cluster --name my-management-cluster --kubernetes-version v1.28.0

```

> Replace 'my-management-cluster' with your desired cluster name.

steps.title: Verify Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks that the Cluster API components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> You should see the Cluster API components running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API components
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Cluster API components and CRDs from your cluster. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if any managed clusters are still present.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API components
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your existing clusters. This step will upgrade the Cluster API components to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get clusters -o json > clusters-backup.json

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Backup existing cluster configurations.
> This command upgrades the Cluster API components.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster CRD is not installed or not available.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'context deadline exceeded'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates that the API server is not reachable or the cluster is not responding.

**Fix:**
Check the network connectivity and ensure the Kubernetes API server is running.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to create cluster: unable to create control plane'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can occur if the control plane components are not properly configured.

**Fix:**
Review the control plane configuration and ensure all required parameters are set correctly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates an issue with the Kubernetes API server's certificate.

**Fix:**
Ensure that your kubeconfig is correctly configured and points to the right cluster context.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kubernetes 1.28, improved error handling in the control plane.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.26.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.11.x, ensure to update your CRD definitions as they may have changed.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get clusters' showing your managed clusters with their statuses.
codeSnippets: helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.4

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster with the necessary tools installed.
